summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier gripe about Apple Notes' single-story 'a' glyph by exploring its surprising historical precedent in the original Macintosh. Kevin Fox pointed out that early pre-release versions of the Mac used a single-story 'a' in Geneva, and Gruber verified using Infinite Mac emulators that System 1.0 actually shipped with it in Geneva 9-point. The post traces this typographic detail through System 1.0 screenshots, the original Mac user manual, and observations from Marcin Wichary about Chicago 12 also having a single-story 'a' in early builds. Gruber clarifies he's not opposed to single-story a's in general — Futura is possibly his favorite typeface — but finds the alternate glyph in modern San Francisco gives Notes an unserious vibe.
key_points: Apple shipped System 1.0 with a single-story 'a' in Geneva 9-point, though Geneva 12 and larger used the double-story version
The original 1984 Mac user manual contains screenshots of pre-release software with icons and glyphs that changed before shipping — and is praised as a remarkably well-crafted document
Marcin Wichary discovered that early screenshots even show Chicago 12 with a single-story 'a', which Gruber considers near-blasphemous
Gruber distinguishes between typefaces designed with single-story a's (like Futura) and retrofitting one into San Francisco for Apple Notes, which he finds gives an unserious vibe
money_quotes: Geneva 9 — the eventual version, with a double-story a — is so intimately familiar to me that looking at those screenshots from System 1.0 makes me feel weird. It's so clearly wrong.
Seeing a single-story a in Chicago feels more blasphemous than that AI-generated image Trump tweeted of himself as the new pope.
What a remarkably good user manual this is — everything from the typography, to the clarity and tone of its writing, to its comprehensiveness is exemplary.
I of course am not opposed to single-story a's. Futura's a is single-story, and Futura, depending on my mood, might be my answer if asked to name my favorite typeface of all time.
I just don't particularly care for the alternate single-story a in San Francisco, and to me it just gives an ever-so-slightly wrong — a little silly or unserious — vibe in the Notes app.
key_insight: The single-story 'a' that Gruber finds irksome in modern Apple Notes actually has deep roots in the original Macintosh, but its brief appearance in early Mac system fonts was corrected before most users ever saw it — suggesting Apple once knew better.
